summary: I enlisted in the United States Navy in November 1965 to avoid being drafted into the Army. I served for two years, mostly aboard the USS Roosevelt, an aircraft carrier. My role involved running supply for the V-2 Division, which handled catapults and arresting gear, and sometimes working on the flight deck. The Navy life was tough but manageable, and I was glad to be in the Navy rather than the Army or Marines during the Vietnam War.

I enlisted in the Navy at Glenview, Illinois, which was the starting point of my military journey. From there, I was sent to Great Lakes for boot camp. Glenview was where I took that first step into the Navy at the age of nineteen, after finishing high school and working as a printer.

After enlisting in Glenview, I was sent to the Great Lakes Naval Training Center for about eight weeks of boot camp. It was my introduction to Navy life and training, preparing me for my assignment aboard the USS Roosevelt. Great Lakes was where I learned the basics before heading out to sea.

I spent my entire two years of Navy service aboard the USS Roosevelt, an aircraft carrier based in Mayport, Florida. We were the first East Coast carrier to go to Vietnam, sailing around South Africa and stopping in places like Cuba and Brazil. My job was running supply for the V-2 Division and sometimes working on the flight deck. The Roosevelt was my home during the war, and I experienced a lot of intense moments there.
cues: first East Coast carrier to Vietnam

summary: Mayport, Florida was the home port of the USS Roosevelt where I was stationed after boot camp. It was from Mayport that we set sail on our long journey to Vietnam, traveling around South Africa and stopping in various ports. Mayport was the starting point of my deployment overseas.

I served during the Vietnam War aboard the USS Roosevelt, which was the first East Coast carrier to head to the conflict zone. We left around June 1965 and arrived late July, flying missions twelve hours a day. The war was intense, and while we were on the ship, we had to maintain the aircraft and keep the flight deck operational. It was a tough time, but I was glad to be in the Navy rather than on the ground.

On our way to Vietnam, the USS Roosevelt sailed all the way around South Africa, stopping in Cape Town on the return trip. We were supposed to get liberty there, but due to political pressure from Senator Chuck Percy about segregation, we were not allowed to leave the ship. It was disappointing because the locals had prepared celebrations for us, but we had to stay onboard for about three or four days.

Before arriving in Vietnam, the USS Roosevelt stopped in Cuba for additional training around mid-1965. This was part of our long journey from Mayport, Florida, to the war zone. The stop in Cuba was brief but important for preparing the crew and aircraft for combat operations.

On the way to Vietnam, our carrier made a stop in Brazil to resupply and rest briefly. This was part of the long trip around South Africa that took about 30 to 32 days. Brazil was one of the few ports of call during that extensive voyage.

When the USS Roosevelt stopped in Cape Town, South Africa, on the way back from Vietnam, we were supposed to get liberty. However, due to political pressure from Senator Chuck Percy about the segregation policies in South Africa, we were not allowed to leave the ship. The locals had prepared parties for us, but we had to stay onboard for three or four days, which upset many of the crew.

Chuck Percy was a senator from Illinois who caused a stir when the USS Roosevelt stopped in South Africa. He raised concerns about the country's segregation policies, which led to us being confined to the ship and denied liberty in Cape Town. While his intentions were to protest segregation, many sailors, including black crew members, were upset because it spoiled what could have been a good visit.

The United States Senate indirectly affected my Navy experience when Senator Chuck Percy from Illinois intervened during our stop in South Africa. His actions, representing the Senate's stance on segregation, resulted in us being confined to the ship in Cape Town. It showed how political decisions back home could impact us even while deployed overseas.

After returning from Vietnam, the USS Roosevelt made deployments to the Mediterranean Sea. It was during one of these Mediterranean cruises that I finished my Navy service and got out. The Med was part of our post-Vietnam operations before I returned home.

My job aboard the USS Roosevelt was running supply for the V-2 Division, which was responsible for the catapults and arresting gear that launched and caught planes on the flight deck. I also sometimes worked on the LSO platform, spotting planes as they landed. The V-2 Division was critical to flight operations, and I was proud to be part of it.

While I was serving, the USS Oriskany caught fire and lost several men. We were nearby when it happened, and it was a sobering reminder of how dangerous life on an aircraft carrier could be. The Oriskany fire was a tragic event that affected many of us in the Navy.

Shortly after I returned from Vietnam, the USS Forrestal suffered a devastating fire and almost lost the ship. The fire started on the flight deck, where planes loaded with bombs exploded in a chain reaction. John McCain was on board and escaped just in time. It was a tragic event that killed many sailors and highlighted the dangers of using old ordnance.

John McCain was aboard the USS Forrestal during the terrible fire that nearly destroyed the ship. His plane was reportedly the first to be hit, but he managed to escape just before it exploded. Though I wasn't on the Forrestal, I knew about the incident and the many lives lost, and it stuck with me.

I graduated from Maine East High School in 1964 before enlisting in the Navy. Interestingly, I ran into several guys from Maine East who also served on the USS Roosevelt with me. One friend and I even went through boot camp together and served throughout the Vietnam deployment on the same ship.
